source_metadata.title_raw: Barbara Harmon - A-18-172 - August 24, 2018 - Arroyo Grande

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Barbara Harmon 
216 S. Mason Street 
Arroyo Grande, CA 93420 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-172 
Dear Ms. Harmon: 
August 24, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact 
when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes 
your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions 
should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit you, an Arroyo Grande City Councilmember, from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to the Brisco Road Interchange Project given that your residence is 
located within approximately 2,100 feet of the intersection at Grand Avenue and U.S. Highway 101 
("U.S. 101 ") which would be improved under two of three design options for the Project under 
consideration? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in those decisions because they would 
not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your real property interest in your 
residence. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR 
The Brisco Road Interchange Project began in the late 1990s. The purpose of the Project is 
"to investigate, identify, and propose potential modifications to the [northbound U.S. 101] on and 
off-ramps at Brisco Road in order to alleviate traffic congestion at peak travel times." Among other 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-172 
Page No. 2 
things, the Project will determine the future location of the northbound U.S. 101 currently located at 
the intersection of Brisco Road and U.S. 101. The Project is a joint venture between the City of 
Arroyo Grande and the California Department of Transportation ("Caltrans"). With respect to the 
Project, the City is responsible for design, technical documents and environmental review, and 
Caltrans is the lead agency under the federal National Environmental Policy Act. 
The City Council will soon decide between the following three design options for the 
Project: 
• 
Option 1: The City would close the existing northbound U.S. 101 on and off-ramps at Brisco 
Road, complete improvements at the intersections of Camino Mercado and U.S. 101 and Grand 
Avenue and U.S. 101, and complete the widening of the U.S. 101 overpass at Grand Avenue. 
The estimated cost of this option is $14,000,000. 
• 
Option 2: The City would relocate the northbound U.S. 101 on and off-ramps at Brisco Road to 
Rodeo Drive, complete improvements at the intersections of Rodeo Drive and Grace Lane and 
Grand Avenue and U.S. 101. The estimated cost of this option is $23,000,000. 
• 
Option 3: There would be "no build," and there is no cost indicated for this option. 
The City Council will also consider adopting findings necessary for the approval of a 
Mitigated Negative Declaration for the Project, select a design alternative for the Project, and 
provide direction to staff on how to budget for the selected design alternative. 
You own your residence, a single-family home located on South Mason Street within a 
residential neighborhood in the portion of the City northeast of the junction of State Route 227 
("S.R. 227") and U.S. 101. Your residence is approximately: 
• 
One and a half miles from the intersection of Camino Mercado and U.S. 101; 
• 
One mile from the intersection of U.S. 101 and Brisco Road; 
• 
4,800 feet from the intersection of U.S. 101 and Rodeo Drive; 
• 
4,150 feet from the intersection of Rodeo Drive and Grace Lane; and 
• 
2,100 feet from the intersection of Grand Avenue and U.S. 101. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act prohibits a public official from taking part in a governmental decision if that 
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's 
financial interests distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 
87103.) An official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act 
are identified in Section 87103. Of those interests, the facts presented indicate that you have a real 
property interest in your residence, assuming you have an interest in the residence worth $2,000 or 


File No. A-18-172 
Page No. 3 
more, and an interest in your personal finances and those of immediate family members, with 
respect to the decisions relating to the Brisco Road Interchange Project. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701(a) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to an official's interest 
that is explicitly involved in the decision at issue. This regulation provides that a decision's effect 
on an official's interest is presumed reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the 
subject of the decision. Under this regulation, an interest is the subject of a proceeding if the 
decision involves any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described 
in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l )-(6). 
Regulation 18702.2 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on 
an official's real property interest. Paragraph (6) of subdivision (a) of that regulation provides that a 
decision's effect on an official's real property interest is material if the decision involves 
construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and 
the official will receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment as a result of the decision. 
The decisions relating to the Project involve the construction of, and improvements to, U.S. 
101 on and off-ramps and street intersections that are various distances from your residence. On and 
off-ramps are adjacent to streets, and street intersections involve streets by definition. Therefore, at 
issue is whether you will receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment as a result of any of those 
decisions. 
In the recently issued Hill Advice Letter, No. A-18-092, we concluded that the Act's 
conflict of interest provisions prohibited the Mayor of Arroyo Grande from taking part in decisions 
relating to the selection of a design option for this Project because those decisions would have a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Mayor's real property interest in his 
residence. Applying Regulation 18702.2(a)(6), we found that the Mayor would receive a 
disproportionate benefit or detriment as a result of those decisions. We further concluded that the 
individual decisions on each design option were incapable of segmentation because they were 
inextricably interrelated. 
We now examine whether you would receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment as a 
result of decisions relating to the Project. 
Option 1 
If the City selects Option 1, the existing northbound U.S. 101 on and off-ramps at Brisco 
Road would be closed, the intersections of Camino Mercado and U.S. 101 and of Grand Avenue 
and U.S. 101 would be improved, and the Grand Avenue overpass of U.S. 101 would be widened. 
The intersection of Camino Mercado and U.S. 101 is approximately one and a half miles east of 
your residence; the intersection of Brisco Road and U.S. 101, the location of the existing 
northbound on and off-ramps at issue, is approximately one mile east of your residence; and the 
intersection of Grand A venue and U.S. 101 is approximate! y 2, 100 feet east of your residence. 


File No. A-18-172 
Page No. 4 
In the Hill Advice Letter, we found that the City's selection of Option 1 would confer a 
disproportionate benefit and detriment on the Mayor. The removal of the existing on and off-ramps 
at Brisco Road would result in the Mayor's receipt of a disproportionate benefit because it would 
reduce traffic congestion during peak travel times in the area immediately southwest of the Mayor's 
residence, and a disproportionate detriment because it would force the Mayor to travel farther from 
his residence to access northbound U.S. 101. 
In contrast, you would not receive a disproportionate benefit as a result of a decrease in 
traffic congestion due to the removal of the existing on and off-ramps located at the intersection of 
U.S. 101 and Brisco Road because that intersection is located more than twice as far away from 
your residence than the Mayor's residence. Furthermore, you would not receive a disproportionate 
detriment from the removal of those on and off-ramps because it would not affect the distance you 
must travel to access northbound U.S. 101, as you can access that freeway via on and off-ramps at 
Grand A venue which are much closer to your residence. 
Moreover, neither the improvement of the intersections of Camino Mercado and U.S. 101 
and of Grand Avenue and U.S. 101 nor the widening of the Grand Avenue overpass of U.S. 101 
would result in your receipt of a disproportionate benefit or detriment. The intersection of Camino 
Mercado and U.S. 101 is even farther east from your residence than the Brisco Road intersection 
with U.S. 101, and therefore, its improvement would not result in your receipt of a disproportionate 
benefit or detriment. Although the intersection of Grand Avenue and U.S. 101 and the Grand 
A venue overpass are only approximately 2,100 feet from your residence, any benefit or detriment 
you may receive from that intersection's improvement or the widening of that overpass also would 
not be disproportionate. Therefore, you would not receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment 
due to the City's selection of Option 1 for the Project. 
Option 2 
If the City selects Option 2, it would relocate the northbound U.S. 101 on and off-ramps at 
Brisco Road to Rodeo Drive and improve the intersections of Rodeo Drive and Grace Lane and 
Grand Avenue and U.S. 101. Your residence is located approximately 4,800 feet from the 
intersection of Rodeo Drive and U.S. 101. 
In the Hill Advice Letter, we found that the City's selection of Option 2 would result in the 
Mayor's receipt of the disproportionate benefits of an improved intersection within only 920 feet of 
his residence and a shorter trip to access U.S. 101 from his residence, as well as the 
disproportionate detriment of increased traffic congestion during peak travel times on Rodeo Drive, 
the street on which the Mayor's residence is located, and Grace Lane. 
Neither the improvement of the intersection of Rodeo Drive and Grace Lane nor any 
increase in traffic congestion on those roadways due to the relocation of the existing on and off-
ramps at Brisco Road to Rodeo Drive would result in your receipt of a disproportionate benefit or 
detriment. Your residence is approximately 4, 150 feet from the intersection of Rodeo Drive and 
Grace Lane, more than four times farther away from that intersection than the Mayor's residence, so 
any benefit or detriment you receive from its improvement would not be disproportionate. Any 
increase in traffic congestion on Rodeo Drive and Grace Lane also would not result in your receipt 


File No. A-18-172 
Page No. 5 
of a disproportionate benefit or detriment because of the substantial distance between those streets 
and your residence. 
Furthermore, because the intersection of Camino Mercado and northbound U.S. 101 is half a 
mile farther away from your residence than the intersection of Brisco Road and northbound U.S. 
101 analyzed above, any benefit or detriment you receive from the improvement of the intersection 
of Camino Mercado and northbound U.S. 101 would not be disproportionate. Although the 
intersection of Grand Avenue and U.S. 101 is only approximately 2,100 feet from your residence, 
any benefit or detriment you may receive from that intersection's improvement, including the 
benefit of the aesthetic upgrades and the detriment of temporary construction impacts, would not be 
disproportionate compared to those received by other residents in the portion of the City northeast 
of the junction of S.R. 227 and U.S. 101. Therefore, you would not receive a disproportionate 
benefit or detriment due to the City's selection of Option 2 for the Project. 
Option 3 
There would be "no build" under the Project if the City selects Option 3. Based on the facts 
presented, this option maintains the status quo. Maintenance of the status quo would not result in 
your receipt of a disproportionate benefit or detriment. 
In the Hill Advice Letter, we found that the City's selection of Option 3 would confer a 
disproportionate benefit or detriment to the Mayor because the selection of that option would 
eliminate the potential for impacts from nearby construction inherent to the other options. Here, we 
have already determined that the construction impacts inherent to Options 1 and 2 would not confer 
a disproportionate benefit or detriment to you if selected. Therefore, the preclusion of those options 
by the City's selection of Option 3 also would not result in your receipt of a disproportionate benefit 
or detriment. 
Thus, you would not receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment as a result of the 
decisions relating to the Project based on the facts presented. Because those decisions would not 
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your real property interest in your 
residence, the Act does not prohibit you from taking part in those decisions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Acting General Counsel 
#i!trlfbr' 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division
